title: A Reply, or an Exercise in Blame?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR omitted the printed salutation and contains several words fractured by page breaks. The salutation and only unambiguous fractures are restored in the Tamil reading copy. Quotations, dates, names, and the chronology of the Tha. Kiruttinan case are preserved as printed. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has replied in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly to the debate on the Governor's Address. There was no setting in which anyone representing the principal opposition parties, the DMDK, the DMK, Puthiya Tamilagam and the Manithaneya Makkal Katchi, could express their views on that Address. As far as the DMK was concerned, the situation was hostile to legislative democracy: no opportunity was given to voice any opinion against the ruling party. As though a Governor's Address had to be placed before the House merely as a matter of convention and could therefore be produced in some perfunctory fashion, that is how it was presented again this year. It announced no new policy or scheme. Every year, a debate on the Governor's Address ordinarily lasts at least five or six days. The opposition is given greater opportunity to state its criticisms and accusations against the government, and the Chief Minister customarily replies to those points at the end. Under this AIADMK government, however, no one may say anything against the rulers. There is room only for praise and garlands of adulation for the Chief Minister. If anyone voices even one opinion against the government, every minister rises, interrupts and speaks recklessly. The Speaker immediately expels from the House the members of the party that criticised the government. If their fellow party members seek to speak in their support, every one of them is expelled too.

Statements made in opposition are not entered in the Assembly record. This is what happens here. How many people can speak in a debate lasting only three days? On one of those three days, the Chief Minister did not even come to the House. Yet she arrived on the third day and delivered her reply, and all the newspapers have delighted in presenting that reply as something magnificent.
One evening newspaper carried a portion of the Chief Minister's reply to the Governor's Address on its front page. Do you know its headline?
"When Karunanidhi Says Alagiri Poses a Threat to Stalin, Why Does He Write to the Prime Minister? Jayalalithaa Asks."

Under that headline, the report quoted Jayalalithaa as saying: "DMK president Karunanidhi has been acting against the government. In an interview with reporters on 28 January 2014, Karunanidhi asked, 'How can it be an offence for me, as party president, to question and reprimand the person who wrote to the police seeking action under the PCR Act against the party's Madurai district secretary, Moorthy?' In other words, he has said that even filing a police complaint against someone who acted unlawfully is wrong. If this is how things are when he is out of power, I leave it to your imagination how law and order would have been maintained when he was in office."

In Madurai, some members of the same party deliberately submitted a complaint alleging an incident that had never occurred against several party members, including a district secretary, in the fond hope that the ruling party would support them. That was an internal party matter. As party president, is it an offence under Amma's "dictionary" for me to summon, question and take action against those who behaved improperly? Is it fair to manufacture a complaint about something that never happened and submit it to the police against people one dislikes over an internal party dispute? Is disciplinary action against them within the party an unlawful act?

Does the AIADMK have no internal disputes? When such disputes arose, was no action ever taken within the AIADMK? What kinds of quarrels arose with the family of the woman described as her family and as one who lived in inseparable intimacy with her? Do the people of Tamil Nadu not know what unlawful measures were taken, using the weapon of ruling-party authority and the aid of the police?

Were extraordinary actions deliberately taken by the police against Coimbatore Ravanan and Mannargudi Divakaran, or were they not? From the time the AIADMK government assumed office in May 2011, pages and pages of reports appeared alleging that all of them had engaged in wrongdoing. The Tamil Nadu police merely watched without acting against them. Then, as soon as Jayalalithaa acted against Sasikala, how intensely were measures taken to suppress every one of them?

What severe measures were taken against Natarajan, the husband of the "sister not born with her"? Was a complaint not suddenly lodged against him, followed by his arrest? Was any proper preliminary inquiry conducted into that complaint? Was the inquiry completed in a single day? If he was arrested the very day after the complaint was made, what was the motive? Why was Sasikala's husband arrested on the day she came to testify in the Bengaluru court?

But how did Sasikala return to her house? Jayalalithaa accused Sasikala and her relatives of wrongdoing, expelled them all from the party and sent them away from the Garden. How, and why, did all of them later receive a new lease of life? Speaking at the AIADMK General Council on 30 December 2011, Jaya declared, "From now on, there is neither association nor relationship with Sasikala's family." Yet Sasikala's statement was repeatedly broadcast on Jaya TV on 29 March 2012.

Natarajan himself said that an attempt had even been made to "encounter" him. To this day, this government has offered no reply. Does Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, carrying such an ugly history on her back, possess any qualification to criticise action taken in the DMK according to the party's rules?

Jayalalithaa's next accusation against me was that I had asked T. R. Baalu to write to the Prime Minister of India about the security of my younger son, M. K. Stalin. Newspapers initially reported it as though I myself had written to the Prime Minister about Stalin's safety. I was startled when I saw that report. Only after I asked how the story had arisen and when I had written such a letter did I myself learn that the DMK Parliamentary Party leader, T. R. Baalu, had written one, not now, but before the present issue arose. It was only afterwards that T. R. Baalu clarified: "In my capacity as leader of the DMK Parliamentary Party, I, T. R. Baalu, wrote to the Prime Minister last December. I handed him the letter during the last winter session of Parliament. Reports now connect my letter with current events and say that the DMK president wrote it. I wish to make it clear that this is untrue. M. K. Stalin will travel throughout Tamil Nadu as the DMK's star campaigner in the coming Lok Sabha election. It is doubtful to what extent the Tamil Nadu police can guarantee his safety. As far back as 2006, near Madurai railway station, it was a member of the Central Reserve Police Force assigned to protect Stalin who saved him from a man who came to attack him with a knife. The Tamil Nadu police took no action then. I sent the letter to the Prime Minister because I did not want the same situation to arise during the coming election. That is the truth."

Even after T. R. Baalu made this statement and the clarification appeared in the newspapers, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa told the Legislative Assembly, contrary to the truth, that I had instructed T. R. Baalu to write to the Prime Minister of India. Jayalalithaa further said: "What position did Karunanidhi take in the Tha. Kiruttinan murder case? It is laughable for DMK members, led by Karunanidhi, who applies one justice to himself, another to his family and yet another to everyone else, to speak about law and order." What position did I take in the case to which Jayalalithaa refers? Was a DMK government in office then? Was it not Jayalalithaa's government? I was shattered when I learned that Pasumpon Tha. Kiruttinan, a former minister, the DMK's commander in the southern region, the man largely responsible for growing the party in Mugavai district after Thennarasu's death, and one who had immense affection for me, had been murdered on the morning of 20 May 2003. In my condolence message on Tha. Ki.'s death, I wrote: "My younger brother whom we called Tha. Ki.; one as dear to me as life itself; have madmen taken your life?

"I cannot believe it, my brother! Was it not only two days ago that you came to Chennai, received birthday greetings from me and your Anni at my home, and draped shawls over us both? Even yesterday, 19 May 2003, at about six in the evening, you spoke to me by telephone from Madurai, explained the party elections in the Madurai region, received my guidance and continued your work. And before that could fade, has this ear-splitting, heart-shattering news arrived? The day before, on 18 May 2003, you brought former AIADMK MLA Suba. Durairaj of the Karaikudi constituency to Anna Arivalayam and inducted him into the party. Even that photograph now stands before my eyes and casts me into a furnace! My brother, how can I conduct this movement after losing people like you? Alas! I tremble; I collapse; I do not possess even the strength to console myself!"

On behalf of party headquarters, General Secretary Professor and Treasurer Arcot Veerasamy went in person to pay respects to the fallen warrior. Thalapathi Stalin, who was then in the United States, heard the news, cancelled his journey and returned from Chicago to Madurai.

As people say, it was cruelty upon cruelty. While all of us were shedding tears of blood over Tha. Kiruttinan's death, the AIADMK government, driven by political vengeance, sent hundreds of police officers into M. K. Alagiri's home in Madurai's Sathya Sai Nagar at about 4.30 a.m. on 21 May 2003, even before Kiruttinan's body had been buried. They said he had to be questioned in connection with the murder and attempted to arrest and take him away. DMK volunteers gathered there, raised the slogan "Down with police tyranny!" and lay on the ground in front of the van to block it. Alagiri himself calmed them all and left with the police.

When reporters questioned him, Alagiri said, "I will face the AIADMK rulers' false case through the courts." The police first took him to Madurai Central Prison, then transported him by van to Tiruchi Central Prison and confined him there.

When reporters asked me about M. K. Alagiri's arrest, I said: "If they have caught the true culprits, it is to be welcomed. But if the action amounts to placing the sin in one place and the blame in another, or trying to bring down two mangoes with one stone, it will allow the real culprits to escape and become an obstacle preventing honest justice." On 24 May 2003, I wrote at length to Prime Minister Vajpayee and Deputy Prime Minister L. K. Advani about the continuing pattern of false cases, political vengeance and abuse of power against opposition parties in Tamil Nadu.

The Hindu of 18 June 2003 wrote: "The sudden transfer of four senior police officers investigating the Tha. Kiruttinan case has aroused suspicion among many. DIG P. Kandasamy, who held additional charge as Commissioner of Police in Madurai, was transferred within twenty-four hours of M. K. Alagiri's arrest. He was moved within three months of assuming office and took charge at Villupuram. Sundaresan, inspector of the Anna Nagar police station adjoining the area where Tha. Kiruttinan was murdered, was also transferred. Until the second week of June, he had not been given an alternative posting. Two key assistant commissioners conducting an intensive investigation of the case, Hariharan and A. Bose, were hurriedly transferred two days ago. They too were given no other posting. It is noteworthy that they had discovered important evidence in the investigation.

"Their sudden transfer has surprised even the special-task officers investigating the apprehension of the culprits in this case. At the same time, he has been promoted as Additional Superintendent of Police and continues to serve in the Tallakulam division. The order granting this promotion was issued in an unprecedented manner never before followed or extended to anyone else." This was what The Hindu reported. Believing that the people would have forgotten all this, Jayalalithaa has flung dust however she pleased!

Speaking about law and order, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa asked what action was taken over the burning of a newspaper office in Madurai under the DMK government; she said every accused person was acquitted and asked whether this was how law and justice had been upheld. Was no action taken against the accused under the DMK government? What is the Chief Minister saying? Is insisting that only a retired judge continue to hear a case, and demanding the appointment of only the person she names as opposing counsel, her idea of upholding justice? Has she forgotten that a Supreme Court judge accused a Chief Minister herself of wrongdoing for insisting that one particular judge alone should hear a case? Who acts against the law while in power? Me, or Jayalalithaa? I shall write tomorrow about the reservation issue in the Teachers Eligibility Test, the power-cut crisis and industrial development, matters on which Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has spoken.
With affection,
M. K.
5 February 2014
